name: Yazilikaya Shrine
description: Yazilikaya, meaning "inscribed rock" in Turkish, is a rock sanctuary located near Hattusa, the capital of the Hittite Empire, in modern-day Turkey. It served as a significant religious center, likely used for ceremonies and rituals connected to the Hittite royal family and the state religion. The shrine is characterized by two main chambers, Chamber A and Chamber B, each adorned with impressive rock reliefs depicting a pantheon of Hittite deities, kings, and mythical figures. The reliefs provide invaluable insights into the Hittite religion, cosmology, and royal ideology. The eastern wall of Chamber A showcases a grand procession of male deities, while the western wall features female deities. Chamber B, smaller and more enigmatic, depicts scenes that are interpreted as related to the underworld, death, and rebirth. Some scholars believe that Chamber B might have been connected to royal funerary rites or rituals associated with the transition of kings into the divine realm. The entire complex is believed to have played a crucial role in legitimizing Hittite rule and connecting the royal family to the divine realm. Yazilikaya stands as a testament to the sophisticated religious beliefs and artistic achievements of the Hittite civilization, providing a tangible link to their rich mythological heritage and the complex relationship between the human and divine worlds.
subtitle: Inscribed Rocks, Sacred Sanctuary

keyMyths.title: The Meeting of Teshub and Hepat
keyMyths.description: The prominent relief of Teshub, the storm god, meeting Hepat, the sun goddess, signifies the central importance of this divine couple in the Hittite pantheon and their role in maintaining cosmic order. This encounter likely formed the basis of important religious ceremonies.

keyMyths.title: The Sword God
keyMyths.description: The figure of the Sword God, depicted in Chamber B, is enigmatic and its significance is debated. Some interpret it as a symbol of the underworld, while others see it as a representation of the king's power and his connection to the afterlife.

sources.author: Bryce, Trevor
